---
title: "LRP83: Within-control crossover — word reading (W), waitlist as own control"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-warning title="Warning: Preliminary model output"}

This model output is preliminary and likely to change as the model evolves and further data are received.

:::

```{python}
# | echo: false
import json
from pathlib import Path

import arviz as az
import pandas as pd

_here = Path(".")
with open(_here / "config.json") as f:
    config = json.load(f)
trace = az.from_netcdf(_here / "trace.nc")
```

## Overview

LRP83 is the [#104](https://github.com/dseinternational/language-reading-predictors/issues/104)
Phase-3 **within-control crossover** — a design-based estimate of the
intervention effect that triangulates the randomised period-1 ITT (LRP52) and the
dose-response (LRP77). It restricts to the **waitlist control arm**, which is off
intervention in period 1 and on from period 2 after crossover, and estimates the
within-child on-vs-off effect on word-reading conditional change. The design
follows Snowling / Gertz / Bayliss et al.

**Estimand.** `beta_on`, the crossover effect, on the same logit scale and with
the same `tau`-style prior as the ITT — so the two are directly comparable.
Outcome: Beta-Binomial post-count of $W$ conditional on its own baseline logit
($N = 79$); conditional change, not a raw change score.

$$
\eta = \alpha + \beta_{\text{on}}\,\text{on}
     + \gamma_{\text{own}}\,\text{logit}(W_{\text{pre}})
     + \gamma_A\,z(\text{age})
     + u_{\text{child}}
$$

where $\text{on} = \mathbb{1}[\text{period} \ge 2]$. A subject random intercept
makes each control its own baseline.

**No period intercepts — by design.** For the waitlist arm `on` is *identical* to
"period $\ge 2$", so a free per-period intercept would absorb the crossover
effect and leave $\beta_{\text{on}}$ non-identified. The smooth time trend is
carried by the linear age term, leaving $\beta_{\text{on}}$ as the discrete jump
at crossover beyond that trend.

## Causal warrant and the maturation caveat

This is **less rigorous than the two-arm ITT** and is a triangulation point, not a
clean randomised contrast. Because `on` and period coincide for the controls,
$\beta_{\text{on}}$ equals the intervention effect **only under a
no-strong-(differential)-maturation assumption**: any non-age-smooth change that
coincides with crossover (a testing or seasonal effect) is absorbed into
$\beta_{\text{on}}$. The linear age term controls smooth maturation; it cannot
rule out a discrete period-2 jump unrelated to treatment.

Read $\beta_{\text{on}}$ **alongside** the randomised ITT (LRP52, between-arm) and
the dose-response (LRP77, all-periods): convergent signs across all three — each
with different assumptions — are the strength of the conclusion, not any one fit.
Note the sign conventions differ: here $\beta_{\text{on}} > 0$ means *on
intervention scores higher*; the ITT $\tau$ is coded on the waitlist indicator, so
*benefit there is $\tau < 0$*.

![Model diagram](model_graph.svg){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

## Shared priors

![alpha](prior_alpha.svg){width=40%}
![beta_on (tau prior)](prior_tau.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_own](prior_gamma_own.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_cross](prior_gamma_cross.svg){width=40%}
![kappa](prior_kappa.svg){width=40%}

## Sampling diagnostics

```{python}
# | echo: false
diag = pd.read_csv(_here / "diagnostics.csv", index_col=0)
diag
```

![Trace](trace_plot.png){.lightbox}
![Energy](energy_plot.png){.lightbox}

## Crossover effect (the estimand)

```{python}
# | echo: false
crossover = pd.read_csv(_here / "crossover_summary.csv")
crossover
```

- `beta_on_logit`: the on-vs-off effect on the logit scale (mean, equal-tailed CI,
  $P(>0)$). Positive = higher word reading when on intervention.
- `ame_prob`: the probability-scale average marginal effect — averaging
  $\text{expit}(\eta_0 + \beta_{\text{on}}) - \text{expit}(\eta_0)$ over the fitted
  control rows, the same construction as the ITT $\tau$ summary so the two are
  comparable.

Expect a **wide interval**: only ~25 control children contribute. The value is
directional triangulation of the ITT, not a precise estimate.

## Posterior predictive

![Posterior predictive check](posterior_predictive_check.png){.lightbox}

## LOO-PSIS

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
print("```\n" + open(_here / "loo.txt").read() + "\n```")
```

## Configuration

```{python}
# | echo: false
config
```
